chunk_summary: Guidance on calcium and vitamin D intake, sunlight exposure, and supplementation for preventing deficiencies, tailored to age, dietary habits, and cultural factors.
learning_objectives: Understand dietary sources of calcium and vitamin D and their role in preventing deficiencies.
Learn how to prescribe sunlight exposure and vitamin D supplementation for different age groups.
Recognize the importance of separating calcium and iron supplementation to optimize absorption.
Identify risk factors for deficiencies in adolescents and provide targeted interventions.
key_facts: Vitamin D supplementation: 400 IU/day throughout infancy.
Calcium-rich foods include milk, curd, paneer, ragi, sesame, soy products, and calcium-fortified foods.
Sunlight exposure: Mid-day outdoor exposure with face, forearms, and legs (if feasible) exposed; sunlight through glass is ineffective.
Separate calcium and iron supplementation by several hours to avoid absorption interference.
Adolescents with low sun exposure and dairy avoidance may require time-limited calcium supplements and risk-based 25(OH)D testing.
Vitamin D supplementation without calcium is insufficient for rickets therapy.
reference_quote: Recommend vitamin D 400 IU/day throughout infancy. For older children, give a concrete sunlight prescription: regular outdoor exposure around mid-day with face and forearms (and legs when feasible) exposed, while balancing sunburn risk and local air pollution constraints. Emphasize that sunlight through glass is ineffective. (Source: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements: Vitamin D fact sheet)
document_key_topics: Calcium and vitamin D dietary sources
Sunlight exposure for vitamin D synthesis
